next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 14

The rental car was a gray Hyundai Elantra that smelled like the previous renter's choices: a pine air freshener clipped to the vent, dried coffee in the cupholder, the stale salt-and-grease trace of a fast food meal that had happened somewhere between here and wherever that person had been going. Layla had booked the tour in a single phone call while Doug was on his lunch break. Three cities, three comedy clubs, three nights. "Warm-ups," she called them. "Before the big show." The Netflix taping was in two weeks. Doug pulled onto I-71 north toward Cleveland and did not feel warm.

He had driven this route to visit Kelsey when she first moved there, before the visits became less frequent, then occasional, then something they planned over text three weeks in advance. She lived twenty minutes from the club. He had not told her he was coming.

Wiley's sat in a strip mall between a Great Clips and a vape shop. Doug went on in an untucked button-down and jeans. For half an hour he did borrowed comedy: pauses he'd studied on YouTube, callbacks he'd diagrammed in a Holiday Inn room, crowd work he'd rehearsed and then lost in the moment. People stayed patient in the specific way audiences stay patient when they're waiting for a person who has not arrived.

Eighteen minutes in, a woman near the back yelled, "Do the toast!" Doug said, "I can't. I'm sober." That got him the only real laugh of the night. Everything after that sounded secondhand, including his thank-you. In the parking lot he called Simone and told her he'd tried to do it like a comedian. "Don't," she said....
open_hooks_to_carry: The Thai place — pad thai and massaman curry, the routine of fourteen years
Kelsey's revelation: the Excel spreadsheet of Megan's failures that Kelsey found at sixteen
prev_tail_excerpt:
...profile before Doug reached his desk. Janet stopped him in the hallway and said, "I read it. Very thoughtful," in a tone usually reserved for eulogies. A woman from accounting whose name Doug could not remember said, "My husband liked the cereal part." Doug thanked them both, sat down, turned on his computer, and opened the claims queue.

Roy was already at his desk. His coffee was half-drunk.

"Morning," Roy said.

"Morning," Doug said.

"I read the headline."

"Just the headline?"

"It's a headline." Roy took a sip of his coffee. "They called you a comedian."

Doug waited.

"You gave one speech, Doug. You were drunk."

Roy turned back to his screen.

Doug looked at his own. Fourteen claims. A burst pipe in Westerville. A chimney fire in Dublin. A woman in Upper Arlington whose sump pump had failed during last week's rain. She'd photographed her basement from four angles. She'd kept the receipt for the pump. Her handwritten description got tighter and more frantic in the margins where she'd added what she'd remembered later.

The phone's red light blinked through the morning. Layla, probably, or a podcast, or somebody who wanted the man from the article. That version of Doug was already easier to handle than the real one. He fit in headlines. He fit in share text. He fit in other people's sentences.

Doug opened the Upper Arlington file. Coverage applied. No exclusions. He wrote the assessment, entered the determination code, and moved to the next screen.

The phone kept blinking. Doug picked it up. It was Layla. He put her on hold and dialed the woman in Upper Arlington.
state_deltas_required: The specific failures of Doug's fatherhood — not absence or cruelty but the insurance adjuster's instinct to document rather than feel
The deepest reckoning — Kelsey reveals the spreadsheet, Doug confronts his specific mode of failure, and the relationship is rawer and closer than it has ever been
